perturbation.original_text: Each Party receiving Proprietary Information will require that all third parties to this Agreement, if any, to which it may give such proprietary information protect the same in accordance with the provisions contained herein. The third party shall be required to execute an agreement with the same provisions as contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party receiving Proprietary Information will encourage third parties to protect the information in accordance with the provisions contained herein. The third party may be asked to execute an agreement with provisions similar to those contained herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will require that' to 'will encourage' and 'shall be required' to 'may be asked' weakens the obligation for third parties to protect proprietary information and execute an agreement, introducing ambiguity and potentially leading to inadequate protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ala. Code § 8-27-1 et seq. - Alabama Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Alabama Trade Secrets Act provides a cause of action for actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the requirement to protect proprietary information by third parties, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation, potentially conflicting with the protection intended by the Act. The original text's mandatory language aligns better with preventing misappropriation, while the modified text's permissive language provides weaker protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Alabama Code § 8-27-3 (2022) - Misappropriation. :: 2022 Code of Alabama :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Code of Alabama 2023 Code of Alabama 2022 Code of Alabama (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Code of Alabama Title 8 - Commercial Law and Consumer Protection. Chapter 27 - Alabama Trade Secrets Act. Section 8-27-3 - Misappropriation. Universal Citation: AL Code § 8-27-3 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Section 8-27-3 Misappropriation. A person who discloses or uses the trade secret of another, without a privilege to do so, is liable to the other for misappropriation of the trade secret if: (1) That person discovered the trade secret by improper means; (2) That person's disclosure or use constitutes a breach of confidence reposed in that person by the other; (3) That person learned the trade secret from a third person, and knew or should have known that (i) the information was a trade secret and (ii) that the trade secret had been appropriated under circumstances which violate the provisions of (1) or (2), above; or (4) That person learned the information and knew or should have known that it was a trade secret and that its disclosure was made to that person by mistake. (Acts 1987, No. 87-669, p. 1195, §2.) Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Alabama may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the protection of trade secrets, creating a contradiction under the Alabama Trade Secrets Act. The original text uses mandatory language ('will require,' 'shall be required'), while the changed text uses permissive language ('will encourage,' 'may be asked'). This weakens the obligation for third parties to protect proprietary information. The law snippet (Alabama Code § 8-27-3 (2022)) addresses misappropriation, stating that a person who discloses or uses a trade secret without privilege is liable if the disclosure constitutes a breach of confidence. By weakening the requirement for third parties to protect trade secrets, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation and conflicts with the protection the Act intends to provide.

perturbation.original_text: Except as provided in Paragraphs 6 and 7 hereof, Proprietary Information disclosed to a receiving Party shall for a period of three (3 ) years from the effective date of thi s Agreement be held in confidence by the receiving Party and not be disclosed to others or used except for the purposes set forth above, without the prior written approval of the disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as provided in Paragraphs 6 and 7 hereof, Proprietary Information disclosed to a receiving Party shall be held in reasonable confidence by the receiving Party for approximately three (3) years from the effective date of this Agreement and should not be disclosed to others or used extensively except for the purposes set forth above, without the prior written approval of the disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be held in confidence' to 'shall be held in reasonable confidence,' 'three years' to 'approximately three years,' and 'not be disclosed' to 'should not be disclosed extensively' introduces ambiguity regarding the level of confidentiality, the duration of the confidentiality obligation, and the extent of permitted use. This creates uncertainty and weakens the protection of proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Alabama's Trade Secrets Act requires protection of trade secrets. The changes make the level, duration, and extent of permitted use of Proprietary Information vague. By introducing 'reasonable confidence,' 'approximately three years' and 'should not be disclosed extensively' weakens the obligation to hold the data in confidence. This would be contradictory to the strict measures usually expected to meet compliance for Trade Secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity that undermines the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating Alabama's Trade Secrets Act. By changing 'shall be held in confidence' to 'shall be held in reasonable confidence,' 'three years' to 'approximately three years,' and 'not be disclosed' to 'should not be disclosed extensively,' the contract weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information. AL Code § 8-27-3 states that a person is liable for misappropriation if their 'disclosure or use constitutes a breach of confidence.' The original language created a clear obligation. The changed language leaves room for interpretation of what constitutes 'reasonable confidence' or 'extensively,' which goes against the intent of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall utilize no less than reasonable care in protecting Proprietary Information received from the disclosing party; such care shall be the same degree of care as though it were to pro tect its own confidential or proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties should attempt to utilize reasonable care in protecting Proprietary Information received from the disclosing party; such care should be similar to the degree of care it gives to its own confidential or proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall utilize no less than' to 'should attempt to utilize' and 'shall be the same degree of care' to 'should be similar to the degree of care' makes the standard of care for protecting proprietary information less stringent and more discretionary. This weakens the obligation and introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: By using 'should attempt to utilize reasonable care' and 'should be similar to the degree of care,' the altered wording does not guarantee the necessary protection required under the Alabama Trade Secrets Act and shifts responsibility with permissive language.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall utilize no less than' to 'should attempt to utilize' and 'shall be the same degree of care' to 'should be similar to the degree of care' weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information, potentially contradicting the Alabama Trade Secrets Act. AL Code § 8-27-3 outlines misappropriation liability for unauthorized disclosure or use of trade secrets. By making the protection of proprietary information more discretionary, the altered text increases the risk of misappropriation not being adequately prevented, which could lead to liability under the statute. The original text ensured a specific standard of care, while the modified text introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of that standard.

perturbation.original_text: Upon written request of a Party who has disclosed Proprietary Information to a receiving Party, the receiving Party shall promptly return all Proprietary Information except that one copy may be retained by legal counsel of the receiving Party as evidence of what was disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written request of a Party who has disclosed Proprietary Information to a receiving Party, the receiving Party will try to return most of the Proprietary Information except that one copy can be retained by legal counsel of the receiving Party as evidence of what was disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly return all' to 'will try to return most of the' and 'may be retained' to 'can be retained' introduces ambiguity about the completeness of the return and weakens the obligation to return all proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, potentially violating Alabama's Trade Secret Act and making it hard to enforce return of confidential documents, especially in cases of misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text, by altering 'shall promptly return all' to 'will try to return most' and 'may be retained' to 'can be retained,' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets. The original contract mandates a clear action ('shall promptly return all'), while the modified version uses less definitive language ('will try to return most'). This weakening undermines the intent of Alabama Code § 8-27-3 (2022), which addresses misappropriation of trade secrets. While the snippet primarily focuses on unlawful acquisition, the weakening of the return obligation makes it harder to enforce the protection of trade secrets, especially in scenarios where misappropriation might be alleged or proven. The change introduces ambiguity regarding the completeness of the return, potentially hindering the enforcement of trade secret protections.
